user_input: What are the three principles of cell theory and who developed it?
retrieved_contexts:
['Section 6: Cumulative Q&A; Review
The following questions and answers are drawn from all sections of this document. They are designed
to test factual retrieval and comprehension across multiple domains.
Q1. What are the three principles of the cell theory?
A: (1) All living things are made of cells. (2) The cell is the basic unit of life. (3) All cells arise from
pre-existing cells. The theory was developed by Schleiden, Schwann, and Virchow in the 19th
century.', 'Section 1: Cell Biology
1.1 The Cell as the Basic Unit of Life
All living organisms are composed of one or more cells. The cell theory, formulated in the 19th century
by Schleiden, Schwann, and Virchow, states that: (1) all living things are made of cells, (2) the cell is
the basic unit of life, and (3) all cells arise from pre-existing cells. Cells are broadly classified into
prokaryotic and eukaryotic types based on their structural complexity.
1.2 Prokaryotic vs. Eukaryotic Cells
Feature

entry/exit.  Osmosis: Diffusion of water across a semipermeable membrane.  Mitosis: Cell division producing
two genetically identical daughter cells.  Meiosis: Division producing four genetically distinct gametes.', "Feature

1.3 Key Organelles and Their Functions
• Nucleus: Houses the cell's genetic material (DNA) and directs cellular activities. Enclosed by a", "century.
Q2. What is the primary function of mitochondria?
A: Mitochondria are the primary site of ATP production through cellular respiration (oxidative
phosphorylation). They are often called the 'powerhouse of the cell.'
Q3. How do prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells differ in terms of genetic material?
A: Prokaryotic cells have circular DNA located in the cytoplasm with no membrane-bound nucleus.
Eukaryotic cells have linear DNA enclosed within a membrane-bound nucleus."]
response: The three principles of the cell theory are: (1) All living things are made of cells. (2) The cell is the basic unit of life. (3) All cells arise from pre-existing cells. The theory was developed by Schleiden, Schwann, and Virchow in the 19th century [Source: rag_study_test_document.pdf, p.9].
reference: The three principles of cell theory are: (1) all living things are made of cells, (2) the cell is the basic unit of life, and (3) all cells arise from pre-existing cells. The theory was developed by Schleiden, Schwann, and Virchow in the 19th century.

user_input: State Newton's Second Law of Motion and give its formula.
retrieved_contexts:
["Section 2: Classical Mechanics
2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
Sir Isaac Newton published his three laws of motion in Principia Mathematica (1687). These laws form
the foundation of classical mechanics and describe the relationship between a body and the forces
acting upon it.
Law
Statement
Formula
First (Inertia)
An object at rest stays at rest; an object in motion stays in motion unless acted on by a net external force
ΣF = 0 → a = 0
Second (F=ma)", "Q4. State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
A: Newton's Second Law states that the acceleration of an object is directly proportional to the net
force applied and inversely proportional to its mass: F = ma, where F is force in Newtons, m is mass
in kilograms, and a is acceleration in m/s².
Q5. What is the formula for gravitational potential energy?
A: Gravitational potential energy is PE = mgh, where m is mass (kg), g is gravitational acceleration", "ΣF = 0 → a = 0
Second (F=ma)
The acceleration of an object is directly proportional to the net force and inversely proportional to its mas
F = ma
Third (Action-Reaction)
For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction.
FII = −FII
Table 2.1 — Newton's three laws of motion.
2.2 Work, Energy, and Power
Work is done when a force causes displacement. The work-energy theorem states that the net work
done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy. Energy exists in many forms — kinetic, potential,", "thermal, chemical — and is conserved in a closed system (Law of Conservation of Energy).
• Work: W = Fd cosθ (joules, J) • Kinetic Energy: KE = ½mv² • Gravitational Potential Energy:
PE = mgh • Power: P = W/t (watts, W) • Efficiency: η = (useful output energy / total input energy)
× 100%
2.3 Gravitation
Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation states that every mass attracts every other mass with a force
proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance", "between their centers: F = G(mImI)/r², where G = 6.674 × 10I¹¹ N·m²/kg². On Earth's surface,
gravitational acceleration g ≈ 9.8 m/s²."]
response: Newton's Second Law states that the acceleration of an object is directly proportional to the net force applied and inversely proportional to its mass: F = ma, where F is force in Newtons, m is mass in kilograms, and a is acceleration in m/s² [Source: rag_study_test_document.pdf, p.4].
reference: Newton's Second Law states that the acceleration of an object is directly proportional to the net force applied and inversely proportional to its mass. The formula is F = ma, where F is force in Newtons, m is mass in kilograms, and a is acceleration in m/s².

The Great Depression began with the Wall Street Crash of October 1929. Global GDP fell by an', '3.4 The Cold War (1947–1991)
The Cold War was a geopolitical tension between the United States (and NATO allies) and the Soviet
Union (and Warsaw Pact nations) following WWII. Key events include the Berlin Blockade (1948), the
Korean War (1950–1953), the Cuban Missile Crisis (1962), the Moon Landing (1969), and the Fall of
the Berlin Wall (November 9, 1989). The Cold War ended with the dissolution of the Soviet Union on
December 25, 1991.

response: The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria-Hungary in Sarajevo on June 28, 1914, triggered World War I. This event set off a chain of alliances that led to a full-scale European war beginning on July 28, 1914 [Source: rag_study_test_document.pdf, p.9].
reference: World War I was triggered by the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria-Hungary in Sarajevo on June 28, 1914, which set off a chain of alliances leading to a full-scale European war beginning July 28, 1914.

Q8. When did the Soviet Union dissolve, and what did this mark?
A: The Soviet Union was officially dissolved on December 25, 1991. This event marked the end of
the Cold War — the decades-long geopolitical rivalry between the USA/NATO and the
USSR/Warsaw Pact.

equilibrium price (or 'clearing price') is where the supply and demand curves intersect — the point at
which quantity supplied equals quantity demanded.", 'which quantity supplied equals quantity demanded.

response:
The four main market structures are:

1. Perfect Competition: Many sellers
2. Monopolistic Competition: Many sellers
3. Oligopoly: Few sellers
4. Monopoly: One seller

They differ in the number of sellers present in each structure, ranging from many sellers in perfect and monopolistic competition, to a few in oligopoly, and only one in a monopoly [Source: rag_study_test_document.pdf, p.8].
reference: The four main market structures are: Perfect Competition (many sellers), Monopolistic Competition (many sellers), Oligopoly (few sellers), and Monopoly (one seller). They differ in product type, barriers to entry, and degree of price control.
